name: DRICE (Detailed RICE) Prioritization
decision_served: Deciding which projects a growth or product team should build next to maximize impact per cycle.
summary: DRICE extends standard RICE scoring by adding a high-fidelity 'Detailed RICE' pass on the shortlisted ideas. Teams first T-shirt-score all ideas on Reach, Impact, Confidence, and Effort, then invest ~30 minutes per top idea to produce a dollar-based impact model and engineering estimate.
key_steps: Collect 5x more ideas than you can build, T-shirt-score each on Reach, Impact, Confidence, Effort
Compute RICE score and sort
Take ~2x capacity as a preliminary shortlist
DRICE each shortlisted idea with a clear hypothesis
Build a bottom-up financial impact estimate
Get a detailed engineering effort estimate
Calculate return on eng investment and finalize the quarter's plan

name: GAIN Framework for Feedback
decision_served: Crafting and delivering feedback that someone will actually appreciate and act on.
summary: An evidence-based feedback framework that reframes feedback around the gain (what you want to move toward) rather than the pain. It translates raw reactions into four elements that increase receptivity and behavior change.
key_steps: Goal: name what both parties want more of
Actions: specify what needs to change
Impacts: explain why it matters to the other person
Next actions: agree who does what by when

name: Core 4 Product Velocity Metrics
decision_served: Measuring and improving how fast and effectively a product/engineering team ships value.
summary: Core 4, from the creators of DORA, SPACE, and DevEx, unifies developer-productivity research into four dimensions held in tension and used together as a balanced system.
key_steps: Speed: how quickly the team delivers
Effectiveness: developer experience (DXI)
Quality: change failure rate and reliability
Impact: time spent on new capabilities vs maintenance
Collect a baseline survey, benchmark, and triage the lowest dimension

name: Racecar Growth Framework
decision_served: Designing and sequencing a startup's overall growth strategy and tactics.
summary: Models company growth as a racecar: a self-sustaining growth engine accelerated and maintained by supporting components. It tells you which component to focus on at each stage of company maturity.
key_steps: Growth engine: the self-sustaining loop driving most long-term growth
Kickstarts: unscalable tactics for the first ~1,000 users
Turbo boosts: one-off pushes that spike growth
Lubricants: tactics that make the engine run efficiently
Mid-stage accelerants: levers explored at scale
Add new engines before the primary one asymptotes

name: Subscription Value Loop
decision_served: Growing a consumer subscription app by compounding LTV/CAC and shortening payback period.
summary: Phil Carter's framework for consumer subscription businesses centers on a clear core value promise that drives a compounding three-step loop. As each step strengthens, LTV/CAC rises and payback period falls.
key_steps: Establish a clear, differentiated core value promise
Value Creation: connect users to value fast and keep them returning
Value Delivery: distribute cost-efficiently via word of mouth, SEO, sustainable paid
Value Capture: convert free users and reinvest revenue
Benchmark each step and target the weakest link

name: Foundation Sprint & Founding Hypothesis
decision_served: Jump-starting a new product or startup by aligning the team on a clear, testable strategy.
summary: Jake Knapp and John Zeratsky's two-day Foundation Sprint produces a Founding Hypothesis: a sentence combining customer, approach, and differentiation. It forces hidden strategic predictions into the open so a team can test them.
key_steps: Define the target customer
Choose the approach and why it is superior
Identify the competition and how you radically differentiate
Assemble the Founding Hypothesis sentence
Use the hypothesis to make a testable promise

name: Strategy Blocks
decision_served: Crafting and building alignment around a product or company strategy.
summary: Chandra Janakiraman's operator's framework turns classic strategy wisdom into a five-step process. It produces a 2-year small-'s' strategy (3-5 strategic pillars plus explicit non-priorities) and a separate 3/5/10-year big-'S' strategy.
key_steps: Preparation: gather behavioral, research, leadership, competitive inputs
Strategy Sprint: a facilitated week to select strategic pillars
Design Sprint: pressure-test the chosen direction
Document Writing: write the strategy doc
Rollout: communicate and align the organization

name: Three-Step Framework for Solving Problems
decision_served: Making sure you are solving the right problem before designing or building anything.
summary: Lenny Rachitsky's framework for nailing the problem statement. It crystallizes the problem, builds team alignment, and keeps the team anchored to it throughout execution.
key_steps: Crystallize the problem: a short, focused, solution-agnostic statement with evidence and success criteria
Align on the problem with team and stakeholders
Keep returning to the problem in every review to prevent scope creep

name: Minto Pyramid Principle & SCR Framework
decision_served: Structuring a persuasive proposal, presentation, or strategy doc for busy decision-makers.
summary: The Minto Pyramid Principle says to present top-down: lead with your recommendation, then arguments, then data. SCR wraps it in a Situation-Complication-Resolution narrative.
key_steps: Situation: state the agreed facts and background
Complication: explain what has changed
Resolution: give the recommendation first
Back the recommendation with supporting arguments
Back each argument with data

name: S.P.A.D.E. Decision-Making Framework
decision_served: Making an important, hard, one-way-door decision with clear ownership and accountability.
summary: Gokul Rajaram's framework balances structure and simplicity for major decisions. It ensures everyone is heard while a single accountable person makes and owns the call.
key_steps: Setting: clarify what is decided, why, when by
People: who is responsible, consulted, approves
Alternatives: lay out all viable options
Decide: stakeholders weigh in, owner decides
Explain: communicate and rally everyone

name: RAPID Decision Roles Framework
decision_served: Clarifying who plays which role in a high-value or recurring decision.
summary: Bain & Company's RAPID framework assigns explicit roles for a decision rather than a project. Best for high-value or high-frequency decisions where role ambiguity slows things down.
key_steps: Recommend: proposes the decision
Agree: must sign off
Perform: will execute
Input: consulted for information
Decide: makes the final call

name: Eisenhower Matrix
decision_served: Prioritizing tasks by separating what is truly important from what is merely urgent.
summary: A 2x2 grid that maps tasks by urgency and importance, helping you act on important work, schedule important-but-not-urgent work, delegate urgent-but-unimportant work, and drop the rest.
key_steps: Urgent + Important: do now
Important + Not urgent: schedule
Urgent + Not important: delegate
Not urgent + Not important: eliminate

name: Mission → Vision → Strategy → Goals → Roadmap → Task
decision_served: Understanding how a team's purpose connects all the way down to its day-to-day work.
summary: Lenny Rachitsky's hierarchy clarifies how mission, vision, strategy, goals, roadmap, and task fit together, and why goals must precede the roadmap so prioritization is impact-driven.
key_steps: Mission: what we are trying to achieve
Vision: what the world looks like when achieved
Strategy: our plan to win (3-5 investments)
Goals: how we measure progress
Roadmap: what to build
Task: the next atomic unit of work

name: Willingness-to-Pay (WTP) Research Methods
decision_served: Determining how to price a product by measuring what customers will actually pay.
summary: Kristen Berman's guide to running a pricing study, covering the four common quantitative WTP methods plus qualitative work. Price is shaped by product description and positioning, not a fixed number.
key_steps: Van Westendorp: price-perception questions for an acceptable range
Becker-DeGroot-Marschak: an incentive-compatible bidding mechanism
Multiple price list: accept/reject a ladder of prices
Discrete/choice-based conjoint: infer WTP from forced choices
Pair quantitative results with qualitative research

name: Evals Framework for AI Products
decision_served: Measuring and improving the quality of an AI product beyond informal vibe checks.
summary: Aman Khan's guide to evals, the systematic way to measure whether an AI system does what it should. It defines three eval approaches and standard criteria applied across each part of an AI system.
key_steps: Human evals: thumbs-up/down or expert labels
Code-based evals: deterministic checks on outputs
LLM-based evals: a judge LLM with a calibrated prompt
Apply standard criteria per system step
Build a labeled dataset, validate the eval, iterate

name: Five Prompt Engineering Techniques
decision_served: Getting consistently better, more tailored results when working with AI models.
summary: Mike Taylor's five day-to-day prompt engineering tactics that work across any LLM, giving the model the specific guidance it needs.
key_steps: Role-playing: instruct the AI to assume an expert persona
Style unbundling: list components of a style, then apply
Give examples (few-shot prompting)
Use clear formatting and explicit structure
Chain-of-thought reasoning for complex tasks

name: First-Principles Thinking
decision_served: Solving a novel problem by reasoning from fundamentals instead of copying others.
summary: Breaking a problem down to its fundamental, undeniable truths and reasoning up from there, rather than reasoning by analogy. Effortful but the source of most genuine breakthroughs.
key_steps: Identify and question the assumptions in the conventional approach
Break the problem down to fundamental truths
Reason upward to build an original solution
Pick carefully what to question

name: Positioning-Based Sales Pitch Structure
decision_served: Building a B2B sales pitch that helps buyers confidently choose you over the alternatives.
summary: April Dunford's sales pitch structure, built because 40-60% of B2B deals end in 'no decision.' A setup frames the market around your point of view, then a follow-through centers on your differentiated value.
key_steps: Open with your unique insight into the market
Lay out alternative approaches and their trade-offs
Define what customers really need and confirm agreement
Demo your differentiated value in that context
Tie every feature to business value

name: The Art of the Pivot
decision_served: Deciding whether, when, and how to pivot a struggling startup.
summary: Lenny Rachitsky's data-driven framework for pivoting, distinguishing quick ideation pivots from hard pivots of a live product, and identifying the two clear signs it is time to pivot.
key_steps: Identify which pivot you face: ideation or hard pivot
Hold a hard conversation at ~3 months, then yearly check-ins
Watch for sign 1: persistent lukewarm interest, low retention, plateau
Watch for sign 2: realizing the idea will never be big enough
For hard pivots, keep one working element and double down

name: Kickstart & Scale a Consumer Business (6-Step Playbook)
decision_served: Building a consumer startup from initial idea through a self-sustaining growth engine.
summary: Lenny Rachitsky's six-part playbook synthesizing the growth stories of hundreds of consumer companies, from idea generation through retention and a working growth engine.
key_steps: INSIGHT: come up with your idea
AUDIENCE: identify your super-specific who
HOOK: craft your pitch
REACH: find early adopters by doing things that don't scale
RETAIN: iterate until enough people stick around
SCALE: build your growth engine

name: Kickstart & Scale a B2B Business (7-Part Series)
decision_served: Taking a B2B startup from idea validation to a scaled growth engine.
summary: Lenny Rachitsky's seven-part B2B counterpart to the consumer playbook, sequencing the major milestones of building a B2B company.
key_steps: Come up with the original B2B idea
Validate the startup idea
Identify your ideal customer profile
Find and win your first 10 customers
Find product-market fit in B2B
Scale your B2B growth engine

name: Ideal Customer Profile (ICP) Definition
decision_served: Identifying exactly which customers a B2B startup should target and sell to first.
summary: A structured method for defining the ideal customer profile for an early B2B company, focusing sales, marketing, and product on the segment most likely to buy, succeed, and refer.
key_steps: Profile firmographics of highest-value customers
Identify the buyer and user personas
Pinpoint the specific pain or trigger
Define disqualifying criteria
Use the ICP to focus targeting and messaging

name: Picking a Wedge
decision_served: Choosing a narrow initial focus that lets a startup enter a market and expand.
summary: A wedge is the narrow, focused entry point a startup uses to win an initial beachhead before expanding to a broader vision.
key_steps: Recognize when too broad a focus dilutes product and GTM
Identify a narrow use case, segment, or feature you can win
Choose a wedge acute enough to attract early users but expandable
Validate the wedge gets real traction
Use the wedge as a launchpad toward the larger vision

name: First 90 Days Quick Wins
decision_served: Making an early, credible impact when joining a new company or team.
summary: A collection of 25 quick-win tactics from top leaders for the first 90 days, emphasizing learning before acting, building relationships, and finding visible early wins.
key_steps: Spend early weeks learning product, customers, and decisions
Meet broadly and build relationships
Identify a small set of visible quick wins
Diagnose the biggest problems and form a point of view
Earn trust before pushing major changes

name: Startup Idea Validation Framework
decision_served: Pressure-testing a startup idea before committing significant time and money.
summary: Todd Jackson's framework for validating a startup idea, drawn from companies like Flexport, Vanta, and LaunchDarkly.
key_steps: Articulate the problem and the specific customer
Talk to many potential customers to confirm acute pain
Test demand with a pitch, prototype, or pre-sales
Look for strong signals of pull, not polite interest
Decide to commit, iterate, or move on

name: Four-Part Naming Framework
decision_served: Choosing the right name for a new product or company.
summary: David Placek's step-by-step naming process from Lexicon Branding. A great name builds trust, signals an original idea, and compounds advantage over time.
key_steps: Build for trust: a name that inspires trust and imagination
Communicate an original idea, never merely describe
Be accessible across languages, cultures, platforms
Run a rigorous generation and evaluation process

name: North Star Metric Selection
decision_served: Choosing the single metric that best captures the value your product delivers.
summary: A guide, based on a survey of 40+ growth-stage companies, for choosing the metric that best captures core value and encodes your strategy.
key_steps: Identify the moment users get core value
Choose a metric that captures that value and predicts success
Ensure teams can actually move the metric
Pair the North Star with supporting input metrics
Treat the North Star as the expression of strategy

name: Kombucha Scale (Decision Triage)
decision_served: Deciding whether a decision is even worth a structured decision-making process.
summary: Gokul Rajaram's quick triage heuristic: weigh a decision's importance and urgency before investing process. Trivial choices are made instantly; reserve frameworks for hard, consequential decisions.
key_steps: Assess how important the decision is
Assess how urgent it is
If low-stakes, decide instantly
Reserve structured frameworks for important, hard decisions

name: Regret Minimization Framework
decision_served: Making a major life or career decision by projecting which choice you'll least regret.
summary: Jeff Bezos's mental model for big personal decisions: imagine yourself at 80 looking back, and choose the option that minimizes regret.
key_steps: Project yourself to age 80 looking back
Imagine the regret you'd feel for each option
Choose the path that minimizes long-term regret
Use it for big, irreversible life/career decisions

name: One-Way vs. Two-Way Door Decisions
decision_served: Calibrating how much rigor and time a decision deserves based on reversibility.
summary: Jeff Bezos's Type 1 / Type 2 distinction: irreversible 'one-way door' decisions warrant deliberate process, while reversible 'two-way door' decisions should be made fast.
key_steps: Classify the decision: reversible or irreversible
For two-way-door decisions, decide fast and empower individuals
For one-way-door decisions, slow down and apply rigor
Avoid heavyweight process on reversible decisions

name: Dory and Pulse
decision_served: Surfacing every voice and reaching a decision fairly in a live meeting.
summary: Shishir Mehrotra's two-part meeting ritual: Dory collects and upvotes questions, and Pulse runs structured votes, equalizing voices and removing groupthink.
key_steps: Dory: participants submit questions, others upvote
Address top-voted items first
Pulse: run a structured vote
Mix variations to fit the decision
Use it live to make decisions in real time

name: RICE Scoring
decision_served: Quickly ranking a large backlog of ideas by expected return.
summary: The industry-standard prioritization process of T-shirt-scoring ideas on four dimensions and computing a comparable score, the first pass before the deeper DRICE analysis.
key_steps: Reach: how many customers experience the idea
Impact: how much it moves the target metric
Confidence: how likely it is to work
Effort: engineering time required
Score = (Reach x Impact x Confidence) / Effort, then sort

name: The Four Growth Engines
decision_served: Choosing which self-sustaining loop will drive the majority of your long-term growth.
summary: Within the Racecar framework, four common self-sustaining growth engines, each a loop where an output is reinvested to produce more growth.
key_steps: SEO: content drives search discovery and more content
Paid ads: ads drive users, revenue funds more ads
Sales: salespeople win customers, revenue funds more sales
Virality: users share the product, friends sign up and share

name: Opportunity Solution Tree
decision_served: Connecting a desired outcome to the opportunities and solutions a team could pursue.
summary: Teresa Torres's visual framework that branches from a target outcome into customer opportunities, then solutions and experiments, keeping discovery anchored to outcomes.
key_steps: Place a desired outcome at the root
Branch into the opportunity space — needs, pain points
Frame opportunities as problems, not disguised solutions
Branch opportunities into candidate solutions
Branch solutions into assumption tests

name: The Magic Loop
decision_served: When you want to advance your career or get unstuck, even with a busy or imperfect manager.
summary: Ethan Evans's five-step career-growth loop builds a collaborative, mutually beneficial relationship with your manager. By repeatedly doing your job well, offering help, delivering, and asking for growth-aligned work, you compound responsibility and trust.
key_steps: Do your current job well and confirm it with your manager
Ask your manager how you can help them
Do what you are asked, even if unglamorous
Ask for work that also grows your skills toward a career goal
Repeat the loop

name: Five-Step Stalled-Growth Diagnosis
decision_served: When B2B SaaS growth has slowed and you need to find the real bottleneck before throwing money at marketing.
summary: Jason Cohen's sequential diagnostic for stalled growth: work through five levers in order, since each later lever only matters if the earlier one is healthy.
key_steps: Logo retention / churn (do you have product-market fit?)
Pricing and positioning
Net revenue retention / expansion
Marketing channels and channel saturation
Target market expansion to an adjacent product or channel

name: Jobs to Be Done
decision_served: When you need to understand why customers actually 'hire' a product so you can build the right thing.
summary: JTBD reframes demand as customers hiring products to make progress in a specific context, surfacing the true competitive set and the forces that drive someone to change.
key_steps: Find the struggling moment and context that creates demand
Map the demand-side competitive set
Understand the six phases of the buying timeline
Interview to reconstruct the real switching story

name: The Four Forces of Progress
decision_served: When deciding whether a customer will actually switch to your product, and what is blocking them.
summary: Bob Moesta's model of the four competing forces acting on every switch decision. A customer only moves when forces pushing toward the new solution outweigh forces holding them in place.
key_steps: Push of the current situation
Pull of the new solution
Anxiety of the new
Habit of the present

name: The Five Components of Positioning
decision_served: When figuring out how to position a product so its value is obvious to the right buyers.
summary: April Dunford's methodology builds positioning bottom-up from competitive alternatives rather than starting with a market category.
key_steps: Competitive alternatives (what you must beat, including status quo)
Unique attributes / differentiated capabilities
Value the capabilities deliver
Best-fit customers who care most about that value
Market category that makes your value obvious

name: Crossing the Chasm
decision_served: When taking a disruptive product from early-adopter traction to a repeatable mainstream business.
summary: Geoffrey Moore's model for the gap between visionary early adopters and pragmatist mainstream buyers. Crossing requires dominating one narrow beachhead segment.
key_steps: Win an early market via a visionary executive sponsor
Pick one narrow beachhead segment
Over-commit to that segment's problem to win reference accounts
Use the bowling-alley model to expand to adjacent segments
Match the go-to-market playbook to each adoption phase

name: 7 Powers
decision_served: When assessing whether your business has a durable competitive advantage worth building toward.
summary: Hamilton Helmer's taxonomy of the seven structural conditions that create persistent differential returns.
key_steps: Scale economies
Network economies
Counter-positioning
Switching costs
Branding
Cornered resource
Process power

name: OKRs (Objectives and Key Results)
decision_served: When a team needs to translate strategy into a focused, measurable goal for the quarter.
summary: Christina Wodtke's approach to OKRs: a quarterly objective paired with ~three key results that answer 'how do we know we succeeded?'.
key_steps: Set one aspirational objective for the quarter
Define ~3 key results that answer 'how would we know?'
Triangulate: a hard number, a quality measure, a dollar measure
Track weekly and improve estimation over time

name: GIST Planning
decision_served: When you want to move an organization from opinion-driven roadmaps to evidence-guided planning.
summary: Itamar Gilad's meta-framework that breaks product change into four layers, integrating lean startup, discovery, and growth methods.
key_steps: Goals: what you're trying to achieve
Ideas: hypothetical ways to achieve the goals
Steps: build-measure-learn loops
Tasks: granular work in Kanban/Jira

name: The Confidence Meter
decision_served: When deciding whether you have enough evidence to commit resources to a product idea.
summary: Itamar Gilad's tool for calibrating how much confidence an idea deserves based on the strength of the evidence behind it.
key_steps: Identify the idea or guess being evaluated
Gather evidence (opinions, data, experiments, validations)
Rate confidence based on evidence strength, not enthusiasm
Decide whether to commit or build more confidence

name: Strategic Narrative
decision_served: When you need a story that aligns sales, marketing, product, hiring, and fundraising around why your product matters.
summary: Andy Raskin's five-part narrative structure, modeled on movies, that defines a movement built around an undeniable shift in the world.
key_steps: Name a big shift from an old game to a new game
Name the stakes (winners already playing the new game)
Name the object of the new game
Show the obstacles to reaching the new game
Show how you help customers overcome them

name: Category Design
decision_served: When deciding whether to compete in an existing market or create and dominate a new one.
summary: Christopher Lochhead's discipline of designing a market rather than just a product. The category king captures ~two-thirds of the category's economics.
key_steps: Choose to design a category rather than capture demand
Craft a point of view that frames, claims, and names the problem
Get product, company, and category right together
Use concentrated 'lightning strikes'
Evangelize the problem inside communities

name: The Four-Step Distribution Platform Cycle
decision_served: When timing an entry onto a new distribution platform for acquisition.
summary: Brian Balfour's model that every new distribution platform follows the same predictable cycle.
key_steps: Step Zero: market conditions are met for a new platform
Step One: a moat is identified, a player races to gather it
Step Two: the platform opens, enabling outsized distribution
Step Three: the platform closes for control and monetization

name: Pre-mortem with Kill Criteria
decision_served: When committing to a project and you want to defend against sunk-cost bias and quitting too late.
summary: Annie Duke's approach pairs a pre-mortem with explicit pre-commitments so imagined failure modes change behavior.
key_steps: Imagine the project failed and identify early signals
Convert each signal into a concrete kill criterion
Pre-commit an action for each criterion
Monitor and execute the pre-committed action

name: Make Time
decision_served: When you want to consistently spend your best attention on what matters instead of feeling reactive.
summary: Jake Knapp and John Zeratsky's four-part daily system for designing time.
key_steps: Highlight: choose one priority moment for the day
Laser: protect focused attention
Energize: manage sleep, food, exercise
Reflect: review what worked and adjust

name: The Design Sprint
decision_served: When you need to validate a big product idea quickly without building the real thing.
summary: Jake Knapp's scripted five-day process to go from a problem to a tested prototype.
key_steps: Map the problem and pick a target
Sketch competing solutions
Decide on the best solution and storyboard it
Build a realistic prototype
Test the prototype with real customers

name: DHM Product Strategy Model
decision_served: When defining what your product strategy should actually optimize for.
summary: Gibson Biddle's model that product strategy means delighting customers in hard-to-copy, margin-enhancing ways.
key_steps: Delight customers (does it create real customer love?)
Hard to copy (can competitors easily replicate it?)
Margin-enhancing (does it strengthen the business model?)

name: GEM Roadmap Prioritization
decision_served: When leadership needs to align on how to fundamentally rank competing roadmap priorities.
summary: Gibson Biddle's framework forces leaders to force-rank three fundamental factors so the whole org shares the same priority order.
key_steps: Growth: year-over-year customer growth
Engagement: product quality, often proxied by retention
Monetization: turning customers and product into revenue
Force-rank the three and get leadership to agree

name: The Three Pillars of Product Strategy
decision_served: When a product leader needs to articulate a complete product strategy others can follow.
summary: Jackie Bavaro's breakdown of product strategy into three connected documents.
key_steps: Vision: the inspiring picture of the future
Strategic framework: target market, definition of success, big bets
Roadmap: the sequenced plan of what to build

name: The PEARL Framework
decision_served: When evaluating a candidate's storytelling answers in a PM interview (or structuring your own).
summary: Jackie Bavaro's structure for answering experience-based interview questions, an upgrade on STAR that adds insight and learning.
key_steps: Problem: the problem worth solving
Epiphany: the insight others missed
Action: what you did
Result: the outcome
Learning: what you took away

name: The Four Product Risks
decision_served: When deciding which risks a product team must de-risk before committing to build something.
summary: Christian Idiodi's taxonomy of the four risks every product team must tackle as a cross-functional sport.
key_steps: Value risk: will people buy/choose it?
Usability risk: can they use it?
Feasibility risk: can we build it?
Viability/business risk: will our business support it?

name: The Adjacent User Theory
decision_served: When a hypergrowth product's growth is slowing and you need to find where the next users will come from.
summary: Bangaly Kaba's theory that growth comes from the users just outside your current circle who are aware of but unable to succeed with the product.
key_steps: Deeply understand who your current users are
Identify the adjacent user who tries but can't succeed
Dogfood the product in the adjacent user's state
Watch, talk to, and visit adjacent users
Fix the experience to enable the adjacent user's job

name: Working Backwards (PR/FAQ)
decision_served: When deciding whether a new product or feature is worth building before committing engineering resources.
summary: Amazon's process of starting from the customer's needs and writing a future press release plus FAQ before any code.
key_steps: Write a customer-facing press release for the finished product
Write the FAQ addressing hard questions
Iterate via concentric-circle reviews up to the CEO
Compare PR/FAQs and fund only the best ideas
Hand the validated idea to product development

name: Three Questions to End Every Meeting
decision_served: When you want a meeting to actually produce forward progress.
summary: Alisa Cohn's closing ritual that captures decisions and accountability so meetings have continuity.
key_steps: What did we decide here?
Who needs to do what by when?
Who else needs to know?

name: The Three Realities of Feedback
decision_served: When giving interpersonal feedback and you want it to land without triggering defensiveness.
summary: Carole Robin's model that every exchange contains three realities. Effective feedback means sharing only what you legitimately know: the behavior and its impact on you.
key_steps: Reality 1: my intent and how I see the world
Reality 2: the observable behavior
Reality 3: the impact of that behavior on the other person
Give feedback only from realities 2 and 3

name: Discover Your Superpowers
decision_served: When you want to identify your true strengths and apply them to your future growth.
summary: Donna Lichaw's method for surfacing your superpowers by mining the stories of your past and present.
key_steps: Collect stories from your past and present
Pull recurring strengths out of those stories
Name and own the superpowers
Transpose and apply them to your future goals

name: Deciding When to Invest in a New Acquisition Channel
decision_served: When you are considering investing in an emerging acquisition channel.
summary: Adam Grenier's caution that a new channel rarely fixes a growth problem, because a changed market may mean you no longer have product-market fit.
key_steps: Assume you no longer have PMF in the new market
Re-validate fit before blaming the channel
Recognize the whole customer base may have changed
Only then evaluate launching a new channel

name: The Growth Model
decision_served: When you need a shared, analytical understanding of how your business actually grows.
summary: Dan Hockenmaier's practice of building an analytical, spreadsheet-based representation of how the business grows.
key_steps: Identify the core growth equation and inputs
Build it as a linked spreadsheet model
Force every input to line up and connect
Use the model to guide deep dives and decisions

name: Growth Loops
decision_served: When designing self-reinforcing distribution into a product rather than relying on linear funnels.
summary: Casey Winters' framing of growth as loops where the output of one cycle feeds the input of the next.
key_steps: Identify the loop type (content, viral, or paid)
Design the loop so output feeds the next cycle's input
Build the loop into the product before reaching PMF
Once a loop scales, hire a specialist to make it 10x better

name: Data-Led Growth (PLG)
decision_served: When adding a product-led growth motion and deciding how to make a free product pay off.
summary: Hila Qu reframes PLG as fundamentally data-led growth: a free product is given away to gain reach and usage data.
key_steps: Give away a free product to lower the barrier
Capture and analyze usage behavior of free users
Identify features that correlate with conversion and retention
Optimize activation and the funnel using that data
Pair PLG with a targeted sales motion where it fits

name: Make the Recommendation (Communicating with Executives)
decision_served: When presenting work to executives and you want your message to land.
summary: Ami Vora's principle that executives hold only a few facts, so the most valuable thing you can do is the work of making a clear recommendation.
key_steps: Assume the executive can hold ~3 facts at once
Do the analysis and come with a clear recommendation
Let the executive add breadth and pattern-matching
Keep the exchange complementary: your depth, their breadth

name: Brain Systems Model for Decision-Making
decision_served: When improving how teams collaborate, build products, and make decisions.
summary: Evan LaPointe's model translates brain complexity into a few stackable systems, picturing the brain as a 'college campus' of departments.
key_steps: Accept that the brain operates as distinct systems
Picture the brain as a campus of departments
Stack the three or four main systems as layers
Apply the model to teamwork, product, and decisions

name: Gathering Independent Opinions
decision_served: When making a group decision and you want to avoid junior voices conforming to senior ones.
summary: Annie Duke's practice of collecting opinions independently before any group discussion, so estimates aren't anchored to the most senior person.
key_steps: Pose the question and collect each view privately
Aggregate the independent opinions
Surface the real spread of disagreement
Discuss openly now that anchoring is prevented

name: Program vs Project Orientation
decision_served: When structuring how teams own work: as enduring program teams or as swarming project teams.
summary: Bill Carr contrasts a project orientation with a program orientation where a standing team permanently owns an area and its controllable metrics.
key_steps: Identify the area of ongoing responsibility
Assign a standing team that always owns it
Have the team think holistically, not project-by-project
Hold the team to controllable input metrics

name: Crafting a Compelling Product Vision
decision_served: When a product leader needs to craft and tell a vision that aligns teams.
summary: Ebi Atawodi's framework treats vision as both a set of elements that make it good and a story you tell to align teams.
key_steps: Define the elements of a good vision
Anchor it in a simple, compelling 'why'
Shape the vision into a story
Use the story to align teams

name: Algorithm vs Human Decision Boundary
decision_served: When building algorithm-heavy products and deciding what the algorithm owns versus what people own.
summary: Adriel Frederick's framework rejects feeding all data to an algorithm with one objective, because algorithms miss long-term effects and intent.
key_steps: Recognize algorithms miss long-term effects and intent
Decide what the algorithm is responsible for
Decide what people remain responsible for
Own the framework for how those decisions get made

name: Seeking the Truth of Your Suffering
decision_served: When you feel burned out or unfulfilled in your career.
summary: Andy Johns' personal process for moving from career burnout toward emotional liberation.
key_steps: Begin with the suffering you actually feel
Seek the truth and root cause of that suffering
Practice self-compassion as you find it wasn't your fault

name: Summarization to Build Alignment and Ideas
decision_served: When leading a discussion and struggling to find a compelling 'why' or big idea.
summary: Anneka Gupta's tactic of bringing many voices into a room and repeatedly summarizing what people say and what it implies for direction.
key_steps: Bring many diverse voices into the conversation
Listen to what each person says
Summarize what people are saying at intervals
Summarize what that means for the direction

name: Hierarchy of Engagement
decision_served: Deciding what to build and measure next when growing a consumer or social product.
summary: Sarah Tavel's three-level model for building enduring consumer products. You must master each level before moving up.
key_steps: Level 1: Growing engaged users who complete the core action
Level 2: Retaining users via accruing benefits and mounting loss
Level 3: Self-perpetuating growth via virtuous loops

name: The Strategy Kernel (Good Strategy / Bad Strategy)
decision_served: Crafting a real strategy instead of a list of goals or aspirations.
summary: Richard Rumelt's definition of what every genuine strategy must contain. Strategy is problem-solving.
key_steps: Diagnosis: identify and name the key challenge
Guiding policy: an overall approach for dealing with it
Coherent actions: concrete, mutually reinforcing steps

name: Strategy Choice Cascade (Playing to Win)
decision_served: Defining a winning strategy by making a connected set of choices.
summary: Roger Martin's five linked questions that together produce a strategy, answered as an integrated set.
key_steps: What is our winning aspiration?
Where will we play?
How will we win there?
What capabilities must we have?
What management systems are required?

name: Four Levels of Product-Market Fit
decision_served: Diagnosing how strong your product-market fit is and what to work on next.
summary: Todd Jackson and First Round's framework for B2B founders. PMF is not binary; it strengthens through four levels across satisfaction, demand, and efficiency.
key_steps: Level 1 Nascent: a handful of satisfied customers won through grind
Level 2 Developing: product does the heavy lifting
Level 3 Strong: repeatable demand, improving efficiency
Level 4 Extreme: widespread demand, satisfaction, efficient delivery

name: The Four Ps of Pivoting Toward PMF
decision_served: Deciding what to change when your product is stuck below product-market fit.
summary: Todd Jackson's companion to the levels framework: when stuck, there are exactly four levers you can adjust.
key_steps: Persona: who you are targeting
Problem: the problem you are solving
Promise: how you describe and position the product
Product: the product itself

name: The Sean Ellis Test (PMF Survey)
decision_served: Measuring whether you have product-market fit before scaling growth.
summary: A research-driven approach to measuring PMF. The core signal is the Sean Ellis question (40%+ 'very disappointed' is the threshold), triangulated with relative-utility and behavioral questions, or 6-12 user interviews.
key_steps: Ask how users would feel if they could no longer use the product
Ask how the product compares to alternatives
Ask a behavioral question about the last tool used for the job
If you can't survey 100+, interview 6-12 users
Look for explicit superior value and genuine enthusiasm

name: The Superhuman Product-Market Fit Engine
decision_served: Systematically increasing your product-market fit score and writing your roadmap.
summary: Rahul Vohra's process for turning PMF into a numeric, optimizable metric, focusing roadmap effort on 'somewhat disappointed' users who like the main benefit.
key_steps: Measure PMF with the 'how disappointed' survey question
Segment respondents into very / somewhat / not disappointed
Ignore not-disappointed; don't over-serve very-disappointed
Target somewhat-disappointed users who resonate with the benefit
Each cycle: half the time on what users love, half on objections

name: Pattern Breakers (Inflections, Insights, Founder Future Fit)
decision_served: Evaluating whether a startup idea has breakthrough potential.
summary: Mike Maples Jr's three forces that underlie breakthrough startups.
key_steps: Inflection: an external turning point that creates new empowerment
Insight: a non-obvious idea for how to harness that inflection
Founder future fit: founders living in the future, suited to the idea

name: The Willingness-to-Pay Conversation
decision_served: Validating demand and prioritizing a product roadmap before you build.
summary: Madhavan Ramanujam's principle that price is a measure of value, so willingness-to-pay must be discussed early.
key_steps: Have the value conversation months before launch
Ask whether customers would pay, without asking the price directly
Classify features by willingness to pay
Prioritize the roadmap around the 20% that drives 80% of WTP
Avoid feature shock; don't give the high-value 20% for free

name: Productize to Segments (Act Differently)
decision_served: Designing products and packaging for distinct customer segments.
summary: Madhavan Ramanujam's segmentation principle: real segmentation is based on needs, value, and willingness to pay, not demographics.
key_steps: Reject demographic/persona-only segmentation
Segment by needs, value, and willingness to pay
Test whether teams can act differently for each segment
Productize and package distinctly for each segment
For startups, launch for the priority segment first

name: The JOLT Effect
decision_served: Closing deals that are stalling due to customer indecision rather than competition.
summary: From Dixon and McKenna's research, 40-60% of B2B deals end in 'no decision' because buyers fear messing up. The fix is reducing the buyer's perceived risk of deciding.
key_steps: Judge the level of indecision early in the deal
Offer a recommendation instead of overwhelming with options
Limit the exploration so the customer trusts your guidance
Take risk off the table — de-risk the deal

name: The Challenger Sale
decision_served: Differentiating yourself in a sales conversation to win complex deals.
summary: Matt Dixon's research on what makes top salespeople different: they teach customers what should keep them up at night.
key_steps: Teach the customer something new about their business
Tailor the message to the customer's economic drivers
Take control of the conversation, including price and timing
Reframe the customer's status quo

name: Becoming Indistractable (Four-Step Model)
decision_served: Regaining focus and doing your most important work without distraction.
summary: Nir Eyal's strategy for focus. Since 90% of distraction starts from internal triggers, you master those first.
key_steps: Master internal triggers — the uncomfortable feelings driving distraction
Make time for traction by timeboxing your values
Hack back external triggers — pings, meetings, interruptions
Prevent distraction with pacts (effort, price, identity)

name: Give Away Your Legos
decision_served: Navigating your role and career as a fast-scaling company outgrows your job.
summary: Molly Graham's advice for thriving in hypergrowth: deliberately handing off your current scope is the path to a bigger career.
key_steps: Recognize that scaling makes your job bigger
Notice the discomfort when someone takes over your scope
Deliberately give away the part of the job you've gotten good at
Move up to building larger systems

name: Radical Candor
decision_served: Giving feedback that helps people improve without being a jerk or being too soft.
summary: Kim Scott's two-by-two framework for feedback: care personally on one axis, challenge directly on the other. Radical candor is doing both at once.
key_steps: Care personally — show you care about the person
Challenge directly — give clear, candid feedback
Avoid obnoxious aggression (challenge without care)
Avoid ruinous empathy (care without challenge)
Avoid manipulative insincerity (neither)

name: What Is / What Could Be (The Sparkline)
decision_served: Structuring a presentation or pitch to move an audience to act.
summary: Nancy Duarte's persuasive presentation structure using contrast between the status quo and a better future.
key_steps: Make the listener the hero; approach with empathy
Establish 'what is' — the current reality
Contrast with 'what could be' — a better future
Oscillate between what is and what could be
End with the 'new bliss'

name: The State Change Method
decision_served: Keeping an audience engaged during a talk, meeting, or presentation.
summary: Wes Kao's technique for sustaining attention by punctuating monologues with 'state changes' every few minutes.
key_steps: Recognize attention spans are only 2-4 minutes
Audit your material for long stretches of monologue
Insert a state change every 3-5 minutes or slides
Use prompts, questions, view switches, or polls

name: Managing Up with TLDR + Context
decision_served: Communicating decisions and updates to senior stakeholders efficiently.
summary: Wes Kao's approach to managing up: lead with the gist, with supporting context optional for those who already agree.
key_steps: Understand what is keeping your manager up at night
Lead with the TLDR — the most important point
Add a clearly labeled 'context' section below
Let readers who already agree stop after the gist

name: Eigenquestions
decision_served: Cutting through a tangle of decisions to the one question that resolves the rest.
summary: Shishir Mehrotra's decision ritual: an eigenquestion is the question that, when answered, also answers the most subsequent questions.
key_steps: List the cluster of questions and decisions in play
Identify which question resolves the most others
Frame and debate that eigenquestion explicitly
Let its answer cascade to dependent decisions

name: Product-Led Acquisition
decision_served: Choosing a growth model where product usage itself drives new users.
summary: Julian Shapiro's reframing of product-led growth: acquisition driven by the product's everyday use, at zero marginal cost.
key_steps: Identify whether using the product naturally exposes non-users
Build incentive structures for users to invite others
Engineer the everyday use case to pull in new users
Prefer this over volatile paid acquisition

name: The Creativity Faucet
decision_served: Overcoming creative blocks when writing or generating ideas.
summary: Julian Shapiro's mental model: drain the obvious, low-quality ideas first before fresh ones can flow.
key_steps: Treat your mind like a faucet clogged with stale ideas
Drain the obvious, mediocre ideas onto the page first
Keep going until the unoriginal backlog is cleared
Continue and the fresh, novel ideas begin to flow

name: The Word-of-Mouth Growth Engine
decision_served: Building word of mouth as a primary, measurable growth channel.
summary: Nilan Peiris's system at Wise for driving the majority of growth through word of mouth.
key_steps: Measure word of mouth by asking customers how they heard of you
Use NPS and its comments to find what drives promoters
Identify the levers that move users up the NPS scale
Deliver a 10x better experience worth sharing
Connect customers emotionally to the mission

name: Hire People Who Have Done the Thing Next (Who Method)
decision_served: Hiring the right person for a role rather than gambling on a resume.
summary: Jonathan Lowenhar's hiring approach: define what success looks like 12 months out, then hire someone who has already done exactly that.
key_steps: Don't start from a generic job description
Envision success: 12 months after the hire, what has changed?
Document that picture of success concretely
Interview for people who have already done those things
Avoid gambling on prestigious logos

name: Working Backwards from the Exit (Seduction vs For-Sale Sign)
decision_served: Positioning a company to be acquired on favorable terms.
summary: Jonathan Lowenhar's framework contrasting two acquisition paths: a weak 'for-sale sign' vs 'seducing' acquirers over time.
key_steps: Recognize the for-sale sign path is reactive and weak
Build relationships with potential acquirers early
Treat it like courtship, not an enterprise sales push
Make the acquirer want a future together

name: Fear Gives Bad Advice (Do the Opposite)
decision_served: Making a hard leadership decision when you feel fear or anger.
summary: Matt Mochary's coaching principle: when gripped by fear, recognize it, check with someone not in fear, and often do the opposite.
key_steps: Notice when you are in fear
Recognize fear is about ego threats, not real danger
Check with someone who is not in fear
Often do the opposite of what fear advises

name: Firing With Grace
decision_served: Letting someone go in a humane way that preserves the relationship.
summary: Matt Mochary's approach to firing: do it with compassion, then become the person's agent to help them find their ideal next role.
key_steps: Recognize losing a job is deeply traumatic
Deliver the news with compassion
Become the person's agent to help them find their dream role
Help them discover and land that role
Use the test: would you still be friends afterward?

name: Work Backwards from the Job After Next
decision_served: Choosing your next career move so it serves a long-term destination.
summary: Nikhyl Singhal's framework for career decisions: treat your career like a product and optimize for the job after next.
key_steps: Define your long-term career end state
Break it into version one, two, three like a product
Optimize for the job after next, not just the next job
Choose the current move in service of that arc
Don't conflate promotion with career

name: The Teen Invitation Heuristic for Viral Apps
decision_served: Choosing a target audience for a consumer app that needs to grow virally.
summary: Nikita Bier's heuristic for viral consumer apps: build for teens, whose habits are malleable and who invite freely.
key_steps: Target teens, whose habits are still malleable
Note invites per user drop ~20% per year of age from 13 to 18
Exploit that teens see each other in person daily
Seed the app school-by-school
Accept that adult-focused apps require paid acquisition

name: J-Curve vs Stairs
decision_served: Setting expectations for how progress feels during periods of change or scaling.
summary: Molly Graham's mental model: progress in scaling environments follows a J-curve where things get worse before they get better.
key_steps: Recognize change often follows a J-curve, not steady stairs
Expect a dip — things get worse before better
Distinguish a J-curve dip from genuine decline
Hold steady through the dip

name: B2B Product-Market Fit Diagnostic
decision_served: Diagnosing where you are on the path to B2B product-market fit and what to do next.
summary: Lenny Rachitsky's five-step ladder for B2B product-market fit: locate yourself on the path from one company loving the product to consistent growth, with benchmark timelines and a stuck-branch diagnostic.
key_steps: Get one company to love your product
Get one company to pay a meaningful amount
Get more than one company to love and pay (3-10 paid customers)
Notice the shift from push to pull (organic growth, cold inbound)
Keep growing consistently

name: The 4-Step Fundraising Process Playbook
decision_served: Running a structured Series A (or seed) fundraise from preparation through closing the term sheet.
summary: Marc McCabe's end-to-end guide treats fundraising as a trust-building exercise broken into four stages: preparation, outreach, navigating the process, and partner meetings plus closing. The core idea is to architect a synchronized process so multiple funds reach a decision at the same time, creating a real market for your equity.
key_steps: Preparation: decide why and when to raise, then build a teaser deck, full deck, forecast, and supporting materials over ~4 weeks
Harden the pitch by testing 70%-complete materials with 4-6 friendly founders or investors
Outreach: build a list of 50-60 funds that can write a lead check and line up warm introductions
Navigate the process: compress all first meetings into a 2-3 week window to keep funds on similar timelines
Track every fund like a project manager and keep dialogue moving with relevant updates
Partner meetings + closing: treat the sponsoring partner as a co-conspirator and get legal review of any term sheet
Plan for ~8-16 weeks start to term sheet and keep at least 8 months of runway

name: Raising a Seed Round 101
decision_served: Deciding whether and how to raise a great seed round as an early-stage founder.
summary: Terrence Rohan and Jack Altman's practical guide to seed fundraising, drawn from 1,000+ rounds and advice from iconic founders. It covers whether to raise venture at all, what to prove first, how much to raise, and how to create FOMO across investors to maximize the odds of a strong round.
key_steps: Decide if venture is right: you want scale, accept ~10-20% dilution, and believe outside capital has concrete upside
Before raising, establish proof of commitment, proof of work, and proof of insight
Size the raise for 24-36 months of runway plus a 25% buffer (typically $2M-$4M)
Plan a compressed 2-3 week pitch window and research every target investor
Prepare a minimal but well-crafted deck or memo and secure powerful warm intros
Start small with angels on a SAFE to build social proof before the lead check
Negotiate mainly post-money valuation and board terms; don't celebrate until funds wire

name: When Should a Startup Raise Money
decision_served: Deciding the right timing to raise meaningful capital and accelerate spend at a SaaS startup.
summary: A clear timing guideline: raise meaningful money and accelerate hiring and growth only after you've found product-market fit. Raising and scaling before PMF causes premature scaling, one of the most common ways startups die.
key_steps: Recognize the three business stages: search for PMF, growth, and maturity
In stage one, minimize raising to preserve dilution and avoid time pressure while iterating toward PMF
Confirm you have genuine product-market fit before scaling spend
Raise in the growth stage to fund hiring, paid channels, and a sales team
Raise when added capital can buy a higher growth rate worth the dilution trade-off
Be deliberate about how much you raise, from whom, and the burn it implies

name: The 'Why Now' Framework
decision_served: Articulating why your startup is uniquely possible or timely right now when pitching investors.
summary: A strong 'why now' isn't strictly essential, but it improves your odds because it can unlock a 10x better product or create a new untapped market need. There are five sources of why now, and your why now can also emerge later as the world changes around you.
key_steps: Understand the two advantages a why now creates: enabling a 10x better product or a new untapped market need
Check the five sources: new technology ubiquity, new data/APIs, regulatory change, behavior change, belief change
Identify which source(s) timing most benefits you
If you lack a strong why now, lean on great execution, a 10x product, and a large market
Remember a why now can emerge later as trends shift in your favor
Don't expect even an amazing why now to save weak execution

name: Activating Your Investor Network
decision_served: Getting maximum value from existing investors after the round has closed.
summary: Sam Corcos's system for treating an investor network as one of a startup's biggest untapped sources of value. It rests on building the right network and then making targeted, time-bounded, specific asks that are easy to act on.
key_steps: Build a great network by optimizing for eigenvector centrality (well-connected investors)
Make good asks: keep them targeted, time-bounded, and specific
Make it as easy as possible for investors to help you
Do the work yourself; don't expect value without putting in effort
Send frequent, structured requests rather than waiting for proactive help
Treat investor outreach as a repeatable, high-volume practice

name: Lessons from 140+ Angel Investments
decision_served: Getting started as an angel investor and deciding how to source and evaluate deals.
summary: Lenny's seven biggest surprises from 140+ angel investments, plus how to get started and what to look for. The central lesson: angel investing is more about access and not missing power-law winners than it is about picking, so build a wide portfolio and become someone founders want on their cap table.
key_steps: Get started via friends, pairing with active investors, syndicates, training programs, or cold outreach
Optimize for access: become someone founders want on their cap table through knowledge, audience, signal, or reputation
Build deal flow by sharing great deals with 3-5 trusted investor friends
Mostly follow high-signal leads, but place ~30% of bets on low-signal deals you have unique insight into
Evaluate startups on pain, market size, evidence of PMF, A+ founders, unfair advantage, and business model
Invest in enough deals (~30+) to catch a power-law winner, with consistent check sizes

name: When to Hire Your First Product Manager
decision_served: Deciding whether and when your growing startup needs its first full-time product manager.
summary: Whether or not you hire a PM, someone must do the three jobs of a PM: shape the product, ship the product, and synchronize the people. Three signs indicate it's time to hire a dedicated PM.
key_steps: Recognize the three jobs of a PM: shape the product, ship the product, synchronize the people
Check whether current team members are both good at and want to do those jobs
Watch for sign 1: frequent bottlenecks where teammates can't ship because of you
Watch for sign 2: frequent misalignment across stakeholders
Watch for sign 3: a burning need to go deep on a problem post-PMF
If the signs are present, hire a full-time PM rather than stretching the team

name: Six Rules of Hiring for Growth
decision_served: Deciding when and how to make your first growth hire and build a growth team.
summary: Elena Verna's six hard-won rules for growth hiring, structured to avoid the most common founder mistakes (hiring a generalist with no growth definition, building growth before data, leading with a Head of Growth). The core principle: define your growth model before you hire for growth.
key_steps: Rule 1: Build a growth model (growth levers and motions) before hiring for growth
Rule 2: Build a data team before a growth team
Rule 3: Don't make Head of Growth your first growth hire
Rule 4: Hire the right growth profile for your specific growth motion
Rule 5: Don't default to chasing external talent over internal candidates
Rule 6: Put growth in the right department for your model

name: How to Hire Your First Growth Team
decision_served: Sequencing and timing the build-out of a first dedicated growth team by company stage.
summary: Elena Verna and Andrea Wang's stage-by-stage guide, built from interviews with 12 top growth leaders, for building a first growth team. Growth connects more people to existing value, so it must come after PMF, and acquisition is almost always the right first goal.
key_steps: Before PMF: develop a growth model hypothesis instead of hiring growth talent
Finding PMF: rely on founder-led growth across product-, marketing-, or sales-led motions
After PMF: form the first growth team, typically around an acquisition goal
Hire generalists with high iteration velocity, a growth mindset, and strong execution
Prefer internal candidates who already have business context for early growth roles
Expand to activation, monetization, and engagement pods as the team earns trust

name: Winning at Early-Stage Hiring
decision_served: Attracting and closing world-class talent as a small early-stage startup.
summary: Five consistent themes that separate founders who hire world-class people month after month from those who struggle. The biggest lever is a captivating vision, reinforced by an A++ early team that compounds over time.
key_steps: Refine a captivating, ambitious vision that makes candidates feel their work would be meaningful
Set an even higher bar for your first few hires to build an A++ early team
Lean on social proof, referral networks, and the hiring flywheel that great people create
Never lower the recruiting bar to fill a short-term need
Treat recruiting as a continuous, founder-led priority

name: Adding a Work Trial to Your Interview Process
decision_served: Designing a work-trial step that gives far more hiring signal than a conventional interview.
summary: Lessons from Linear, Automattic, 37signals, Gumroad, Auth0, and PostHog on running work trials, where candidates complete a project representative of the actual role. Trials give dramatically more signal on real performance and on how much a candidate wants the job.
key_steps: Define a work trial: a project representative of the real work the role involves
Choose where to sit on the spectrum from at-home project to co-working in the real codebase, based on company size
Design trial projects that mirror day-to-day work and produce gradeable output
Slot the trial into the overall interview process and decide whether and how much to pay candidates
Evaluate performance against the skills and judgment the role actually requires
Use the trial to also gauge how much the candidate wants to work at your company

name: How to Interview Product Managers
decision_served: Structuring a PM interview process and deciding which skills to test for.
summary: A complete blueprint for a PM interview process: recruiter screen, hiring-manager screen, a full-day interview combining a project with 1:1s, a blind-vote panel, and reference checks. It pairs the structure with the six core IC PM skills to test and tips for closing offers.
key_steps: Run a recruiter phone screen to confirm minimum requirements and org fit (optional)
Run a hiring-manager phone screen to test a couple of the most important attributes
Run a full-day interview: a project plus three to six 1:1s, each testing one key PM skill
Hold a post-interview panel where each interviewer blind-votes Strong No / No / Yes / Strong Yes
Test the six core IC PM skills, including communication and collaboration
Do reference checks, then craft a compelling offer and work hard to close it

name: How to Determine Your Activation Metric
decision_served: Quantitatively pinning down the activation milestone that predicts long-term retention.
summary: A three-step process for finding the activation metric that is causal for retention, not merely correlated. It moves from brainstorming candidate 'aha' moments to regression analysis to experiments that confirm causality.
key_steps: Brainstorm and explore usage data for potential 'aha' moments in the user journey
Run a regression analysis to find which moments correlate with an inflection in retention
Run experiments to test whether increasing that moment causally increases retention
Use qualitative interviews and surveys when historical usage data is thin
For multi-player B2B products, identify activation per job-to-be-done or workspace setup behavior
Pick the milestone that is causal for retention as your activation metric

name: How to Measure Cohort Retention
decision_served: Accurately calculating, visualizing, and reporting on cohort retention.
summary: Olga Berezovsky's deep dive into the formulas, SQL, and visualizations needed to measure cohort retention correctly. The first and most consequential step is defining what 'active' means for your product, since retention is both the most important and least understood metric.
key_steps: Define 'active' carefully, ideally using a main user action rather than visits or logins
Choose the retention type (n-day, unbounded, bracket) that fits your product's usage cadence
Build cohorts and compute retention with correct formulas and SQL
Visualize retention as cohort tables and curves, watching for when curves flatten
Account for differences between consumer, B2B, and B2C definitions of retention
Iterate on retention reporting over months to reach accuracy

name: The Most Important Marketplace Metrics to Track
decision_served: Choosing which KPIs to focus on and optimize when building a marketplace.
summary: Lenny's recommendation, drawn from his Airbnb experience and a dozen marketplace founders, of the four most important metrics for any burgeoning marketplace. Fill rate is the ultimate measure of marketplace health because it bakes in supply quality, availability, and conversion.
key_steps: Track fill rate: the percentage of intentful sessions that convert
Track bookings growth: completed transactions per week or month
Track supply growth: new active supply per week or month
Track GMV growth: dollars flowing through your system per week or month
Also watch universal metrics like retention, user growth, and payback period
Narrow focus to the two or three metrics that most drive your marketplace's health

name: The Most Important Consumer Metrics to Track
decision_served: Selecting which metrics to focus on for a consumer (B2C) product by business model.
summary: The metrics that matter most for a consumer product depend heavily on how it makes money. This guide breaks consumer businesses into five types and recommends a focused starting set of metrics for each.
key_steps: Identify your consumer business type: trial subscription, freemium subscription, ad-based, marketplace, or DTC
For trial subscription, focus on trials growth, conversion, retention, revenue, CAC, and payback
For freemium, focus on user growth, conversion to paid, retention, and virality
For ad-based products, focus on engagement, audience growth, and revenue per user
Treat the suggested metrics as a starting point, not the only answer
Narrow to the two or three metrics that most directly drive your business

name: The Most Important Bottom-Up SaaS Metrics to Track
decision_served: Choosing which metrics an early-stage bottom-up (PLG) SaaS startup should track.
summary: A priority-ranked set of metrics for early-stage bottom-up SaaS founders, divided into pre-revenue, post-revenue, and additional buckets. The guidance is to start with a few metrics and add more over time rather than tracking everything at once.
key_steps: Pre-revenue: track retention (user, logo, L7/L30)
Pre-revenue: track virality within an organization (invite rate, invite conversion, virality factor)
Post-revenue: track MRR and revenue growth
Add metrics like activation, conversion, and expansion as the business matures
Pick the right tools to track and visualize these metrics
Start with a few metrics in priority order rather than all of them at once

name: The Most Important Consumer Subscription Metrics to Track
decision_served: Choosing which metrics to track for a consumer subscription business.
summary: A consumer subscription business has to nail six things, from acquiring users to delivering profitably, and the most important ones differ across Content, Software, and Physical Goods. This guide maps the key metrics to each of the six buckets.
key_steps: Acquire new users sustainably and track acquisition metrics
Get new users to quickly experience value (most important for Software)
Make sure enough users continue to find value (most important for Content)
Make sure enough users decide to pay (free/trial to paid conversion)
Make sure enough users continue to pay (retention and churn)
Deliver the product or service profitably (most important for Physical Goods)

name: SaaS & Consumer Metric Benchmarks
decision_served: Judging whether a given metric (activation, churn, retention, growth, conversion, payback, waitlist) is good for your type of business.
summary: A benchmark reference family answering the recurring 'what is a good X?' question across the core SaaS and consumer metrics. Each benchmark is segmented by business type (B2C, B2B SMB, B2B enterprise) and price point, with GOOD / GREAT / OK thresholds drawn from large surveys and panels of growth experts.
key_steps: Identify which metric you need to benchmark: activation, churn, retention, growth rate, free-to-paid conversion, payback period, or waitlist conversion
Segment by business type (B2C, B2B SMB, B2B enterprise) and price point before comparing
Compare against GOOD, GREAT, and OK thresholds rather than a single number
For payback period, target shorter for B2C and progressively longer for SMB and enterprise
For monthly churn, translate the rate into how fast you churn through your user base
Use the benchmark to decide whether a metric is on track for growth or needs intervention
